text: The robotic-assisted low anterior resection is underway, focusing on dissecting the posterior mesorectal plane. The surgical field shows the use of an endorector, resembling a sponge, to retract the small bowel by instilling saline for added weight. On the right side of the screen, a robotic retractor is visible, while a vessel sealer is on the left, used to achieve hemostasis with diathermy. The surgeon continues dissection on the anterior mesorectal plane, retracting the bladder anteriorly for better access to the retroperitoneal rectum.

The assistant uses a suction device to remove excess blood and fluid from the pelvis, while also providing additional retraction with a laparoscopic retractor. The surgeon divides the rectum using a robotic stapler device, followed by securing the remaining mesorectum with a hemolog device due to the stapler's limited reach.

The surgeon inspects the proximal resection margin, which appears clear, and washes out the pelvis. The distal resection margin is secured. Dissection continues along the left side of the pelvic brim, with the small bowel positioned below the endorector for optimal retraction. The laparoscopic retractor is used to retract the sigmoid colon medially. Dissection into the diverticular abscess is underway, with counter traction aiding in determining the dissection plane. The left ureter is visible and identified to prevent injury during the procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects deeper into the abscess cavity, avoiding the sigmoid colon and major vascular structures. The robotic system provides enhanced optics for precise visualization. The assistant uses a suction device to remove pus from the cavity, preventing contamination of the peritoneal cavity. The surgeon simultaneously retracts and dissects, while the assistant clears fluid from the surgical field, facilitating optimal identification of anatomical structures.

The surgeon skeletonizes the inferior mesenteric artery using the vessel sealer, achieving hemostasis as they divide the artery along the mesocolon inferiorly and around the left pelvic brim. The diseased sigmoid colon is visible, and the assistant uses an anal dilator to check for strictures in the rectum, allowing for further distal resection of the rectum.

The surgeon clears the mesorectum and additional fat around the staple margin using a laparoscopic 60mm stapler. The entire rectum is resected with one stapler. A circular stapler is introduced into the rectostump, with the surgeon retracting the rectostump for optimal positioning. The previously resected sigmoid colon is attached to the anvil of the circular stapler, and a staple anastomosis is performed.
The surgeon cuts an additional suture using laparoscopic scissors. The use of endocyanin green fluorescent dye, injected intravenously, helps visualize the vascular supply of the anastomosis.
